fields.name: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)
fields.definition: One of the most common neurodevelopmental disorders. ADHD affects attention span and concentration and can also affect how impulsive and active the person is. Behaviours associated with ADHD occur more frequently and more severely than is considered typical for a person's age or developmental level, persist over time, and negatively affect the person's family, social, school, or work life. Studies have shown prevalence rates ranging from 1% to 13% among young people. ADHD is three to four times more common in boys than girls.
fields.example: An adult with ADHD may struggle to sustain attention during meetings, frequently lose important items, and feel restless even when physically still.

fields.name: Inattentive Symptoms (ADHD)
fields.definition: A cluster of ADHD symptoms centred on difficulties with attention and focus. A person may be diagnosed with ADHD if they have displayed six or more of these symptoms for at least six months. Inattentive symptoms include: not paying attention to details or making careless mistakes, difficulty staying focused, not seeming to listen when spoken to, failing to follow through on instructions or finish tasks, difficulty organizing, avoiding tasks that require ongoing thinking, losing things, being easily distracted, and frequent forgetfulness. In adults, the core inattention symptoms generally persist even when hyperactivity and impulsivity soften.
fields.example: Frequently missing details in work documents, leaving tasks unfinished, and losing track of what someone is saying mid-conversation.

fields.name: Hyperactive and Impulsive Symptoms (ADHD)
fields.definition: A cluster of ADHD symptoms centred on excessive physical activity and difficulty controlling impulses. A person may be diagnosed with ADHD if they have displayed six or more of these symptoms for at least six months. Hyperactive symptoms include: fidgeting and squirming, leaving one's seat when expected to sit still, running or climbing excessively (or feeling restless in adolescents and adults), talking excessively, difficulty playing or working quietly, and being constantly in motion. Impulsive symptoms include: difficulty waiting for a turn, blurting out answers before questions are finished, and interrupting others' conversations or activities. These symptoms generally soften or change by adolescence, though they may persist in altered form.
fields.example: Standing up during meetings, blurting out comments before others finish speaking, and having difficulty waiting in line.

fields.name: Executive Function and ADHD
fields.definition: A set of mental processes that help people plan, focus, remember instructions, and manage multiple tasks. Executive functions include working memory, cognitive flexibility, and inhibitory control. ADHD significantly impairs executive function, making it difficult to initiate tasks, organize, manage time, regulate emotions, and follow through on intentions. CAMH notes that occupational therapists can help patients review basic life skills and create organizational plans, and that digital technologies can help reduce clutter and manage information, reflecting how central executive function difficulties are to the lived experience of ADHD.
fields.example: Knowing a deadline is tomorrow but being unable to start the task, or frequently forgetting commitments despite meaning to keep them.

fields.body: A clinician applies DSM-5 criteria to determine whether the patient has ADHD symptoms. For adults, DSM-5 requires at least five symptoms of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity (reduced from six in the earlier DSM-IV). The clinician takes a careful developmental history, reviewing childhood records, school report cards, and information from family members or partners. Assessment tools such as the Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale and the Weiss Functional Impairment Rating Scale help document symptom severity and baseline impairment. Conditions that commonly co-occur with or mimic ADHD, including anxiety, depression, personality disorders, and substance use, must be evaluated carefully. Some patients may have developed coping strategies (such as spending excess time on homework or relying heavily on assistance) that mask symptoms, making diagnosis more complex.
fields.name: How ADHD Is Diagnosed in Adults
fields.example: A well-educated professional may not show obvious signs of ADHD because they developed intense compensatory strategies earlier in life, requiring careful exploration of their developmental history.

fields.name: Causes and Risk Factors (ADHD)
fields.definition: The most likely cause of ADHD is genetics. Children born into families with a history of ADHD are more likely to be diagnosed than those without family history. Attempts to link parenting style, early television exposure, and environmental hazards to ADHD have not produced conclusive evidence that they cause the disorder. ADHD is understood as a neurodevelopmental condition, not the result of poor discipline or lifestyle choices.
fields.example: A child diagnosed with ADHD whose parent also had significant attention and impulsivity difficulties throughout their own childhood.

fields.name: Psychoeducation as First Step in ADHD Treatment
fields.definition: Psychoeducation is the recommended first step in treating ADHD. It involves helping the patient understand their condition, directing them to reliable resources, and addressing questions at every visit. CAMH emphasizes that even patients responding well to medication will deteriorate if they do not also make lifestyle changes. Patients may want to rush to starting medication immediately, but clinicians are encouraged to ensure adequate education first. Knowledge reduces dependence on the clinician, and a patient who follows through on the treatment plan demonstrates commitment to the process.
fields.example: A newly diagnosed adult spends their first sessions learning how ADHD has affected their life patterns before any medication is discussed.

fields.name: Stimulant Medication for ADHD
fields.definition: Stimulant medications are the primary pharmacological treatment for ADHD. They include methylphenidate-based products (such as Concerta and Biphentin) and amphetamine-based products (such as Vyvanse and Adderall XR). Long-acting formulations are generally preferred, with short-acting stimulants used for augmentation when needed. Before starting medication, the clinician ensures the patient is medically stable and reviews cardiovascular risks, since all ADHD medications can increase blood pressure and heart rate. Dosing starts low and increases gradually until target symptoms improve. ADHD medications also have a protective role in reducing the risk of self-medication with other substances.
fields.example: A clinician starts a patient on a low dose of a long-acting methylphenidate product and increases gradually until the patient can reliably sit down and manage daily tasks like paying bills.

fields.name: Psychotherapy and Support Strategies for ADHD
fields.definition: Cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT) can help people with ADHD build self-esteem, reduce negative thoughts, and improve problem-solving and self-control skills. Beyond CBT, CAMH identifies several additional support strategies: occupational therapy to review basic life skills and create organizational plans, ADHD coaching to build accountability toward goals, vocational assessment to support workplace accommodations, electronic strategies to reduce clutter and manage information digitally, and community support networks where patients and their partners can learn from others' experiences. People with ADHD often develop belief systems based on a lack of accomplishment, and long-term therapeutic relationships provide an anchor of stability.
fields.example: An adult with ADHD works with an occupational therapist to reorganize their daily routine using digital reminders, while attending a support group where their partner gains insight into the condition.

fields.question: How many ADHD symptoms must adults show for a diagnosis under DSM-5?
fields.answer: DSM-5 requires at least five symptoms of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity for adults. This was reduced from the six-symptom threshold required by the earlier DSM-IV. Symptoms must be present in more than one setting and must cause meaningful impairment in functioning.

fields.question: Why is ADHD often missed in adults who are well-educated or high-achieving?
fields.answer: CAMH notes that patients who are well-educated or employed in high-level positions may have developed unusual coping strategies, such as spending excess time on homework or relying heavily on assistance from others. These strategies can mask the core symptoms. A partner or parent may suggest ADHD while the patient dismisses it. Bright individuals may not have early school records describing problems, making a careful developmental history essential. Clinicians are encouraged to look beyond surface-level functioning and explore whether the person's achievements came at an unsustainable cost.

fields.question: How do ADHD symptoms change between childhood and adulthood?
fields.answer: The core childhood symptoms are inattention, impulse control problems, and motor hyperactivity. By adolescence, impulse control difficulties and motor hyperactivity generally soften or change in how they present, but inattention typically remains. The natural course of ADHD is to develop secondary anxiety and depression by adolescence, but ADHD remains the primary diagnosis. Many patients who had only inattentive symptoms are likely missed entirely.

fields.title: ADHD and Comorbidities
fields.body: ADHD rarely travels alone. CAMH notes that the natural course of untreated ADHD is to develop secondary anxiety and depression by adolescence, with ADHD remaining the primary condition. The co-occurrence of personality disorders is also high. ADHD should be considered in patients presenting with bipolar II disorder, concurrent addictions to catecholamine-active substances (such as cannabis and cocaine), and other impulsive and anxiety disorders. What appears to be a separate condition may actually be a secondary consequence of ADHD. One in five youth with ADHD receives an antidepressant prescription, compared to one in twenty without ADHD, illustrating how frequently co-occurring conditions appear alongside the primary diagnosis.

fields.question: Is ADHD caused by bad parenting, too much television, or environmental factors?
fields.answer: No. CAMH states that while there have been attempts to link parenting style, early television exposure, and environmental hazards to ADHD, there is no conclusive evidence that any of these cause the disorder. The most likely cause is genetics. Children with a family history of ADHD are more likely to be diagnosed than those without. ADHD is a neurodevelopmental condition, not a product of upbringing or lifestyle choices.
